So, I'm going to take you through the entire straight line system from front to back and give you one training that sort of takes you through all the steps in the syntax and I'm going to try to position it in a different way than I've ever done it before. So I basically can tell, you know, so you sort of give you a new perspective on it and hopefully will make you even more effective. All right. So that being said, let me go back to the night that I indented the straight line, which was in 1988 and the month was October and what happened was I was in a rough spot because I stumbled upon an untapped niche in the market, which was selling $5 stocks to the richest 1% of Americans and for whatever reason, no one ever tried that before. Okay. And when I take it, just move it, move it by, move it like there. Yeah. You need too much in my double vision. Yeah. It's more and more like more, more, more, more. Thank you. Thank you. I got to get this set guys. I'm sorry. Just over there so you're not right. Exactly. That's good. All right. Here we go. So what happened was is I had stumbled upon this niche and when I tested the idea myself, the results that I got and I got with Danny is on my dream upon it was so staggering, so amazing that I decided to shut down Stratton the way it was and reinvent the firm. You see, at the time Stratton was selling penny stocks to average moms and dads, right? Penny stocks are stock that's basically under a dollar, 10 cents, right? And we're selling to mailman, people that worked in like a secretary or just not rich people, right? So without any real savings, certainly not people will financially sophisticated and when they would invest in a stock was more like buying a lot of a dollar and a cheap and put a couple hundred bucks in it was buying on hype and bullshit. That was that it wasn't a sophisticated investment made by a sophisticated investor. Now even at this point, I had a system of training sales. I had one. I even did some tonality training, right? I was very, very adept at this. And what happened was when I launched the firm with these 12 young kids and we started selling penny stocks, they were doing really well. The average kid was making 10, 15,000 bucks a month. I had my star salesman Danny making the journey. He was making 30, 40 grand a month, right? And I was making a ton of money myself as a broker and also managing the whole office, right? And it came up with this idea to start calling rich people. Now just to understand, I'm not going to get into the technical side of how I uncovered this niche. Which was selling $5 stocks to the richest 1% of Americans. I was kicking ass. I'm kicking ass. I had a close rate of about 50%. Danny's close rate was 30%. So I'm at 50%. Danny's at 30%. And I shared this program with the other 12 kids after one month. You know what their close rate was? Fucking zero. They hadn't closed a single sale in 30 days. And we're calling the same people selling the same stock, same qualifier. Did you get it? I'm like, how could this be happening? It literally boggled my mind. I was already giving two sales training sessions per day, two per day. Every morning, I give a sales training session. And then in the afternoon, I would give one as well. And here's the thing. The reason I structure this program with daily motivations is because what I learned from that experience is that the daily interaction, the daily pump up, the daily reinforcement of skills is the difference between success and failure for any sales person. Seriously, without that daily anchor for someone, it's really, really difficult to go it alone. So I've noticed throughout these, I started it there. But what I noticed throughout the years after is that as soon as you take away that factor, the results drop. Anyway, so here we are. I'm like, it's so simple. Every sale is the same. And they're like, what? And I can tell from their, you know, their, their speciality, like every sales, not this way. Every sales different. Every sale is the same. And I'm like, watch and all of a sudden some idea pops in my head. It's in the form of a straight line. And I draw this long, thin straight line. I'm going to the center of the whiteboard just like this. And I place a big fit X on either end. And I said to these guys, what? I said, guys, this is your open where the sale begins. And this is your close where the client says, yeah, let's do it. And you open up the sale. And by the way, guys, back in the beginning when you were selling penny stocks, every once in a while, you get one of those perfect lay down sales where everything you'd say, everything you do, the client was like, yes, yes, yes, yes. And it's almost like a pre sold person right from the very start all the way up to the time that you asked for the order for the first time. And they say, yeah, let's do it. Almost like an objectionless close. So essentially that in my mind was the perfect straight line sale. So this is the perfect straight line sale. And I want to stop here and explain what I mean. The shortest distance mathematically between any two points is what a straight line. So if you're trying to close someone, you're not going to purposely try to delay it. You're not going to take a circuitous path to close on you want to close as quickly and efficiently as possible. If you could design a perfect solution yourself, right? It would be let's take the fucking long way home. You take the short way, right? So that's what I meant by the perfect straight line sale. It's basically one of those rare sales where the client is seems to be almost pre sold from the moment he enters the encounter. So everything you say, everything you do, he's like, oh yeah, that sounds good. Oh yeah. And you say, does that make sense? He goes, oh yeah, it's perfect. And you tell him a bit more exactly, he goes, oh, great. Yeah. You say, now getting started is very simple. He's like, oh, wow, that sounds good. And you ask him, he goes, great, where do I sign up? You say, oh, right here and hit me. Here's my credit card. That's the perfect straight line sale. And I said to these guys, you know, back when you were selling penny stocks to average moms and pops every once in a while, you would get one of these perfect laid down sales where they were pre sold almost before they walked in. But in the real world of sales, that's typically not what happens. You see really in the real world, what happens is you want to keep your prospect. Yeah, your goal would always be you want to not make things go longer than they have to. You'd love for things to stay on the straight line where you basically are in control the whole time and you say, yes, yes, yes, yes, yes, right. So that's what you would love to happen, right? Of course you would. But in the real world, your client, your prospect tries to take you off the straight line. They interrupt you. They have questions. They have concerns. They'll go off the Pluto. They interrupt you as you're trying to move them from the open to the close. So what you have in selling are these healthy boundaries above and below the line. These are boundaries that represent. So you just slow this down again. What these boundaries represent is how far the conversation can stray from that perfect situation where you're basically doing all the talking. See when you're on the straight line directly on the straight line that represents you the salesperson doing all the talking and the client sitting and saying, aha, oh great. Wow. Yes. Oh my God. That's so good. Wow. Really great. Oh, awesome. And you ask your salesperson, that's what happens when you're on the line, right? But that's fantasy land, right? Although people in consultative, someone will try to pretend as well if you just be a consultative since it's a fucking joke, right? It doesn't really work. Since it's not all, right? So what happens in the real world is they're going to interrupt you. They have questions. They have concerns, right? They go off the Pluto. They'll try to ramble on, right? So these healthy boundaries, what they represent is how far the sale can stray off this perfectly straight line while you're still in control, meaning it doesn't spiral off into a call as Pluto. Meaning you've gone off to Pluto talking about the price of tea in China, things that basically are not germane to the sale, or down here to your anus, which is not a good place to be for anybody, most of us, right? Most salespeople, right? The point is, is that you want to stay inside these boundaries. Now, when you're, and we stop one more time here. So you really understand what that means. Inside these boundaries, so you're off the straight line. So this spot right here, right here and here. This is where the magic actually happens. Now, when you saw me deconstruct the sale, that last week I did a deconstruction of my own sale, right? So let's say the entire, this is your entire straight line from open to close, right? You're going to get a boundary here and your boundaries here, right? This is your first four seconds where you have to, I'm going to just go through this separately. Your first four seconds, you must take control of the conversation by being perceived as an expert. That's number one. I'll get, I'll go back to the second, right? And then you want to use that control to start gathering intelligence and gathering intelligence means asking questions, following certain rules of prospect and certain rules of influence that allow you to uncover what someone's needs are, what their values are, what their beliefs are, what their hierarchies of value is, what their pain points are. It allows you to uncover all of that without breaking your point, without pissing them off. In fact, when you're doing this right, you want to, you're asking these questions here, right? And you're actually building master report, you're building report. I'll show you how that later, right? So when you ask questions, you're actually building report. So these first three steps, number one, you must take control of the sale. That's your first step. And I'll get back to how I said, right? Number two, you want to use that control, not the talk, talk, talk, talk, talk. You don't want to be a talking head. People hate that, right? You want to use that control to start asking questions. So you can identify their needs, their beliefs, their values, their pain points, right? So you want to then gather, call gathering intelligence. And then you want to ask those questions in a certain way and engage in what's called active listening. So when they respond like, uh-huh, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, yep, got it. You can engage in that active listening so you now build massive rapport on both the conscious and unconscious level. You get that? So these are the three steps. Now watch what I'm going to do. I'm going to take this part right here and let's make that the whole straight line. This is just straight line. I was going to magnify this one section here of intelligence gathering. This whole thing is intelligent, right? Every time you ask a question, so tell me how long you've been living in the area. You've moved forward on the line. You ask another question, okay? And, oh, do your kids go to school? You have any kids you have, okay? Where do they go to school, right? What is your wife do? Every time you ask a question, it's directed. It has a purpose. And that purpose means that you move down the line. You're not asking questions for the sake of asking questions. This is called goal-oriented communication, meaning the goal is we want to move someone down the straight line and close them. That's your goal. So when you ask someone a question, what's actually happening is that question moves you down the straight line. Or else don't fucking ask it. Unless that question gives you some intelligence that you need that allows you to keep going forward. Because if you didn't have this intelligence, you couldn't go forward. You couldn't close the deal. Don't ask it. You don't ask questions for the sake of sounding smart or wasting time. You don't do that. You ask questions because they have a purpose. They have a meaning. And they give you some intelligence that allows you them to move forward and close the deal. So in essence, each question you ask during your intelligence-having phase allows you to move more down the line, more forward, forward, down the line. And what happens is every time you ask someone a question, what can the prospects say? What are the possibilities? Well, they can do A. They can answer you perfectly. So let's say you say, tell me, how long are you living in the area for? Now that question, you are directly on the line. You are on the line right on it. Like, why are you on the right? The prospect says, oh, I've been here for four years. Where are you now? You are still directly on the line. Bingo. He's kept you stayed on the line. He's given you the perfect response. He's not wasting any time. He's told you exactly what you need to hear. So now you can move forward to your next question. OK, great. And you marry a single. Just making questions up, right? And he says, oh, I'm married. Let's just say now, watch. How can you answer this question? He could say, oh, I'm married. That's one answer. So then, again, if you answer it perfectly, which means he gives you the exact correct answer you're looking for, check. You move forward again. But let's say he does something else or she does something else. Because this is what typically happens when you're selling. You ask a question. So tell me, how long are you living in the area for? That's your question. And you move down the line. I've been living here for about three and a half, and I've been living here for, say, three, four years. But before that, I used to live over in Riverdale. Because I moved over here because the next thing you fucking know, the guy starts to spiral off to Pluto. He starts using a digital compound answer. And this is what almost always happens when you're gathering intelligence. You ask someone a question that will give you an answer. But the answer starts to meander off the straight line. It's not just what you ask them. You have to answer it all. So that's one possibility. They won't answer it all. We have to stop. We have to ask that question again. You can't go forward without getting your answer. But the point is, is they can either answer it perfectly. They can answer it. And then meander, well, they can just say, I'm not comfortable answering that question for whatever reason. They just not refused to answer. Those are your possibilities. Well, the third, this one's very rare. They just won't answer. They'll typically just dodge it. And what they'll typically do is they'll answer the question and then start spiraling out because it ignited something in their memory where they just feel that, no, let me volunteer some new information. And before you know it, where do you live comes out to be, oh, yeah, I moved up here because I like duck hunting. And I, you know, my, my, I have a duck, there's a great duck hunting up here in the northern England. And he's actually happy when I was training the company of many years back, right? And next thing I know, all right, next thing I'm listening to the call, the prospect is going on and on for five freaking minutes about the beauty and wonder of duck hunting in northern England. This is actually happening on a call for a currency trading product. Somehow a question, how long you've been trading for, how long you've been trading currency for ended up with, I love duck hunting. And I'm not making this shit up. And it actually happens all the time. So what happened is the guy starts talking about duck hunting. And you know what the product salesman does? Oh my God, duck hunting. I love duck hunting. It's my favorite. And next thing you know, they start spending 15 minutes talking about going and shooting some innocent fucking ducks in northern England and making bucks. That's like stuff. And I'm, and then, and then at the end, of course, the salesman closed the deal. I was like, damn, I thought I was so close. No, you weren't. You've never fucking closed. You lost the sale and you started talking about duck hunting. Here's the deal. Experts don't do that. Experts don't have time, the inclination, they're trained too well. They're not going to waste their time talking about things that are not germane to the sale. So what do you do when someone interrupts you? Well, you can't say, whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa, listen, buddy, you're going off the Pluto here. You're wasting my fucking time. I'm not a straight line expert here. And us straight line experts, we, we know that building rapport is not about talking about duck hunting in the hinterlands of northern England. That's repulse. That's not really rapport building, right? We know rapport is more about the aha, yuck. It's about body language, looking at someone's eyes, listening actively to their responses after you ask them very smart questions. See, straight line experts know that. But if you were to actually say that to your prospect, like, whoa, stop that stuff. Don't stop talking about fucking duck hunting. All right, you're wasting my time here. I'm here to sell you a currency product. Is that going to work very well? No, you're going to lose that sale. You'll break rapport and you'll be done. Right. So how do you get around that? Because remember in this little microcosm, what's our goal here? Let's look at this one intelligence gather, right? Let's look at the phase into itself, just intelligence gather. The goal is we have to get to the end. We have to finish our intelligence. We can't move to the next step in the syntax until we're done gathering all the intelligence we need. We simply can't go forward on the straight line, right? You can't try to close someone until you know what their needs are, what their values are, what their pain points are. That's how you close effectively, right? So the point is, is that while you can't say to someone, buddy, stop. I don't do this rambling and going off talking about the price of tea in China. I'm an expert. So here's what you do. When you ask someone a question, this happens all the time. You say, so tell me how long you're living in the area for. And they say, oh, I've been living here for about four and a half years. Yeah, because before that, I originally came from the southern part of the country here because I really enjoyed duck hunting and I found out the thing. And then next thing you know, they're spiraling off the Pluto. So what do you do? You, if you're when you're in personal when you're like, uh-huh. Oh, mm-hmm. Uh-huh. Wow. Uh-huh. Mm-hmm. You listen. Uh-huh. Great. It's called listening actively. Uh-huh. Yup. Got it. Wow. That's pretty cool. And let them spin out. And then when they're done, when they finally spun out and they finished off that ramble, you're like, oh, wow, it's totally cool. Now watch where they are. So they've spun out here and you just take them right back to the point in the straight line where you left off and you ask the next question in sequence. That's what you do. And once you master this one tactic, it makes your life so much easier. Because what happens is people are always going to spiral out. They're going to ramble on. And people do that to me. I actually use it as a rapport building moment because I'm like, oh, wow. Oh, my God. It's so cool. Really? Oh, great. Wow. But what I don't do is I don't jump into the night and say, oh, my God, I love the fucking too. But you love shotguns? I love shotguns. You like Spanish travel? I want to be an astronaut. That's fucking rapport too small for that shit. That's not where rapport is. Repor is on the unconscious level and it's based on two things. Number one, that you can. You actually care about them. You're not just there to make a fucking commission. You care about the outcome. You get them what they want. And number two, you're just like them, but not in the sense that you like what they like, but that you process the world the same way. You speak it generally the same rate of speech, the same level of voice. You enter their world where they are. You don't come in like if it's just someone's like a slow talking Southerner. I'm like, hey, Jordan, don't I'll slow my patterns down. Okay. And through their world where they are rapport is based on that. And most of all, so that you care that you care. You're not just there to make a commission, which is what most people think about salespeople. All right. So going back to this first night, I said to these guys, don't you guys get it? Every sales the same watch. It's a straight line. And I drew that long thin straight line. And you know, here's the deal guys, you know, I don't know if you ever read the book called thinking bro rich. If you haven't read this book, read it. It's worth reading. It talks about this thing called the collective unconscious and the like all that there is. It's like sort of the ether out there. Now you guys know I'm not a woo woo person, right? I don't believe in the shit about I'm very scientifically grounded, but I do believe that there are these windows of clarity that certain people tend to have these open up. And when they open up in your life, if you really jump through with a vengeance, you'll have access to information that you never had access to before. It's like when you hear about a rock group, like, you know, that they have this one year they made the most amazing music. The album is like the music is holy shit. And they suck forever. Right. I mean, that's what I took on a window of clarity. Well, for me, this, this what I don't know whether it was the anger, the frustration, the fear of my billion dollar idea, having to be abandoned and go back to selling paints or whatever it was, something in the trigger, this window of clarity to open. And all of a sudden I had access to information and ways of explaining things I never had even thought of before. And all happened this night. All right. And I looked at my guys and said, let me tell you this guys, when you, when you, when you're talking to rich people, when you talk to people who are educated, people with person in a position of power and authority, you got three seconds. Okay. Actually, it was funny. You got four seconds. I'm going to go back to that way with Harvard University. You got four seconds to establish three crucial things, four seconds. And I'll tell you exactly what they are. And if you don't establish these things in those first four seconds, you're done. You can't close anybody. They looked at me like, well, I said, in those first four seconds, you must be perceived as being number one. Sharp as a tack, sharp as a tack. Number two, I can almost write again. Enthusiastic as hell. And number three, an expert in your field, an authority figure, an expert in your field of force and record, sharp as a tack, enthusiastic as hell, an expert in your field. You got four seconds to accomplish those three things or else you're done. You can't close anybody. See, what I realized in here, and I kind of gave this to you in the beginning very quickly. I kind of laid the foundation to this. I said to you, I said, guys, what is so odd about it? I don't get it. I'm doing it. Danny's doing it. Why can't you do it? And they said, there's too many objections. There's thousands. Yeah. So, yeah, thousands. Oh my God, so many. And they keep interrupting us. We can't even get our pitches off. And I'm like, wait a second. They can't get their pitches off. It's sharp because I'm like, usually, like when I'm selling, I'm getting the same objections they're getting, but I get them at the end after I ask for the order for the first time. Who's going to object to me before I even ask for the order? Why would you say, let me think about it. Well, let me call you back to bad time. I got to talk to my local broker before I told you to buy me. It doesn't make sense. Yet my brokers were being abused and batted around by the rich people to the point where they couldn't even get a legitimate pitch off. And that was when it hit me. And I realized as to what it was, if there was a certain way that I had of talking, a certain way I had of moving my body, the something I was doing was was in person. My body language and my tone out a certain way that when people would lay eyes on me, I hear my voice over the phone. Instantly they knew there was something different about me. I was not the average bear and same thing for Danny. Here's the interesting with Danny. When I trained Danny, he was the first person I trained had a cell stocks. All right. And he actually struggled for a couple of days. And then once he got it, he just forget it. He took off like a rocket. He was unbelievable. But he struggled for it. But once he got it, it was just like second nature to him. Second nature, right? There was some way that we had I taught Danny of tonality, confidence. And here's the thing. Danny was a very educated guy from a wealthy family. His dad was a very famous doctor, the kidney king of a, of a, was a Brookville hospital in, in, uh, in New York, Brookdale, Brookdale hospital. So Danny came from a very educated, very successful family. He was used to being around rich people's whole life. He wasn't intimidated. He knew he kind of intuitively knew to walk, to walk, talk, to talk. Yet my 12 Schmendricks, right? They'd get in the phone with these rich people and they were completely intimidated, blown away. And they were not coming across being sharp as a tack, enthusiastic as health and expert in there. So they weren't getting back across in those first four seconds. And because of that, what was happening is rather than the prospect deferring to you and saying, all right, you're an expert, you're sharp. I will essentially defer and you control of the sale. Cause that's what we do in the position in the presence of it as we defer and control to an expert. What they were doing is they were trying to grab control. Like this guy's a fucking idiot. I'm not going to let him control. They will back my people around because they were not being perceived the right way. See, here's the deal. The reason these strings, these three things are so crucial is because what they chunk up to in the real world, in the mind of your prospect, they say, this is a person worth listening to. You're a person worth listening to. And then even one level above there, because this person, the salesperson can help me achieve my goals, can help me get what I want in life. That's why they listen to you. They don't listen to you so you can get what you. Look, kids listen to like an early commission or sell you. They listen because they want you to solve a problem and they believe you can. See, we have been conditioned since we a big kids to seek out experts to resolve all problems and remove our pain. Start it back when we were little kids. Our parents took us to the doctor and they were dressed a certain way. They had stethoscope on my jacket diplomas on the walls and these doctors were very special people. Even our own parents would defer to the doctor. And when the doctor asked you a question, what did you do? You answered truthfully, forthrightedly. You didn't bob and weave. You didn't say, so tell me how long the problem. How long you had a problem before the doctor? You don't do that, right? You answered the doctor fully and answered why? Because he's got a diploma. He's spent many years studying how to make sick people feel better. He's earned the right to ask you questions. You owe it to him and you should respect him because he's earned that right. And this is an equation that we all run unconsciously. It happens like we see someone, we lay eyes on them. In those first two seconds, we see them, we rip them apart and we put them back together based on how we perceive them. If the perceives being sharp as a tack, enthusiastic as hell, an expert in their fields, then we say, you know what? I will defer. I'll hand this person control. I'll allow them to ask me questions because they can probably help me resolve my problems. That's what happens. Conversely, if in those first four seconds, they perceive as being dull as dishwater, a disinterested bore, and a stone-cold fucking novice, good luck trying to close one. They will try to take control. And that was what was happening with my first 12 Stratonites. Essentially, the sale was over before it ever started. They had no shot of closing because they weren't being perceived the right way. That makes sense. I know it does. So the thing is, and I mentioned it for a while, you know, how many seconds? Three seconds, four? Well, many years later, by the time it turned out I was wrong, I said you have four seconds to establish these three crucial things, right? 2015, Harvard and I could get a study, a very detailed study, you can Google it, right? And had to have influence and persuasion and first impressions in business. You know what they found? They found that it's not, it's not four seconds. It's not four seconds to make that first impression. It's five seconds. I was off by a second. I'm sorry. But I could beat them by, what, 20 years almost. The point is, is that I was off by a second. Maybe I was right there wrong. They tested this stuff out and here's what else they found. You want to hear something even more staggering? Not only is the first impression that all important first impression, it happens. They said five seconds, not four. But if you make a negative first impression, it takes you seven subsequent impressions, seven times to change someone's mind about you. I know about you. I don't get seven chances. I have one. When I get into a sales encounter with someone, I know, oops, I fucked up. Let's try the second. Let me try third four. They're not going to give you seven shots. You got one shot to show your stuff. So you must be perceived in exactly the right way. But how? And how do you go about doing that? What is it? I mean, think about it. Is it the words that you say? I mean, what would you say?
